audit_status.source_base: Greek 1 Corinthians text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for love as surpassing way, gifts without love, love's qualities, partial knowledge, faith/hope/love.

verses.restored: If I speak in the tongues of humans and of messengers, but do not have love, I have become sounding bronze or clanging cymbal.
And if I have prophecy, and know all mysteries and all knowledge, and if I have all trust so as to move mountains, but do not have love, I am nothing.
And if I distribute all my possessions, and if I hand over my body so that I may boast, but do not have love, I gain nothing.
Love is patient; love shows kindness; love does not envy; love does not boast; it is not puffed up.
It does not act disgracefully, does not seek its own, is not provoked, does not calculate the harmful.
It does not rejoice over injustice, but rejoices together with the truth.
It bears all things, trusts all things, hopes all things, endures all things.
Love never falls. But prophecies will be made inactive; tongues will cease; knowledge will be made inactive.
For we know in part, and we prophesy in part.
But when the complete comes, the partial will be made inactive.
When I was a child, I spoke as a child, thought as a child, calculated as a child; when I became a man, I made inactive the things of the child.
For now we see through a mirror, in enigma, but then face to face; now I know in part, but then I will know fully, just as I also was fully known.
But now remain trust, hope, love, these three; and the greatest of these is love.

translation_notes: Agape is the governing center for all gifts, knowledge, sacrifice, and discipline in 1 Corinthians.
Love is not sentimental softness; it is the durable communal reality without which spiritual power becomes noise.
